one_line_takeaway: For convex models, in-distribution unlearning is dimension-independently cheap (constant fraction removable via ERM + output perturbation, beating differential privacy), but out-of-distribution/adversarial forget data can stall unlearning below retraining cost unless a robust trimmed-mean training variant is used -- which the paper introduces as the first provable defense against unlearning slow-down (DoS) attacks.
key_finding: Approximate unlearning is fundamentally EASIER than differential privacy for in-distribution deletions: simple ERM + output perturbation can unlearn a constant fraction Omega(n*sqrt(alpha)) of the data with NO dependence on model dimension d, whereas DP-style 'lazy' unlearning capacity shrinks with d (validated in Figure 1a where DP-SGD error grows near-linearly with d while Algorithm 1 stays flat). But this breaks entirely for out-of-distribution forget data -- a single adversarial sample can make Algorithm 1's unlearning slower than full retraining (Proposition 2) -- and the proposed robust Algorithm 2 restores a constant-fraction Omega(n) deletion capacity with near-linear time/space and is up to ~10x faster at unlearning than the non-robust Algorithm 1 in the OOD experiment (Figure 1b), giving the first provable defense against unlearning slow-down attacks.
core_contribution: A theoretical characterization of the utility-vs-time/space-complexity trade-offs of APPROXIMATE machine unlearning, split into two regimes of forget data. (1) In-distribution (ID): the authors show that empirical risk minimization with a single output-perturbation step (Algorithm 1) achieves a DIMENSION-INDEPENDENT deletion capacity of Omega(n*sqrt(alpha)) -- i.e. a constant fraction of the dataset can be removed at fixed error alpha -- resolving an open question of Sekhari et al. (2021) (whose best prior capacity decayed as ~1/d^{1/4}) and establishing a tight separation from 'lazy' differential-privacy-based unlearning, whose capacity degrades polynomially with model dimension d. (2) Out-of-distribution (OOD): they INITIATE the theoretical study of forget data that can deviate arbitrarily from the retain distribution, prove (Proposition 2) that the ID procedure can fail to unlearn even a SINGLE adversarial sample because unlearning-time complexity blows up, and propose a new robust noisy gradient-descent variant (Algorithm 2) using coordinate-wise TRIMMED-MEAN gradients that provably amortizes unlearning time to near-linear, independent of the forget data. This OOD robustness is presented as the first theoretical guarantee against slow-down / denial-of-service poisoning attacks on certified unlearning (Marchant et al. 2022). The paper also introduces 'computational deletion capacity' (max deletions within a time budget T) alongside the utility deletion capacity of Sekhari et al.
threat_model: Two distinct adversarial concerns are formalized. (a) PRIVACY / confidentiality: unlearning is defined as (q,epsilon)-approximate unlearning via Renyi divergence of order q between the unlearned model U(Sf, A(S)) and a model retrained without the forget set, U(empty, A(S\Sf)); semantics mirror differential privacy -- an adversary with arbitrary auxiliary information cannot confidently distinguish the unlearned model from one that never saw the forget data. This is the 'right to be forgotten' (GDPR) motivation. (b) AVAILABILITY: an adversary submits deletion requests whose forget data is out-of-distribution or adversarially crafted so as to inflate the cost of certified unlearning, forcing retraining -- the slow-down / DoS attack of Marchant et al. (2022). Algorithm 2 is designed to certifiably bound unlearning time regardless of the forget data, defending against this availability attack. The paper does NOT force a data-poisoning-of-predictions frame; its adversary attacks either indistinguishability (privacy) or unlearning efficiency (availability).
limitations: Strong theoretical assumptions limit direct applicability to deep networks: results require mu-strong convexity, L-smoothness, and a UNIQUE global empirical minimizer (non-convex coverage is limited to special cases such as PCA and matrix completion). The Lipschitz-based ID bound can incur a large Lipschitz constant R (scaling with dimension on bounded domains) or exclude basic tasks like unconstrained least squares. Empirical validation is small-scale and mostly synthetic linear regression; no large models or standard vision/NLP benchmarks in the main text. A unified UPPER bound on deletion capacity across joint utility/time/unlearning budgets remains open (only a utility-side upper bound is known). Space-complexity analysis is largely omitted for simplicity. Algorithm 2's trimming parameter is capped at ~half the data (tau <= ~f, f <= n*min{1/3, 12mu/(5(L-mu))}), bounding how much OOD data can be removed.

datasets_used: Synthetic linear / least-squares regression (main experiments, Figure 1): in-distribution setting uses 10,000 samples with f=20 randomly chosen forget samples; out-of-distribution setting uses 1,000 samples with f in {1, 0.1n, 0.45n}, OOD forget data created by shifting labels by a fixed offset
Real-data validation referenced in Appendix F (details beyond the extracted main body; Pace & Barry 1997 spatial-regression data is cited)
quantitative_results: In-distribution utility deletion capacity of Algorithm 1 (GD) + output perturbation: Omega(n*sqrt(alpha)), independent of dimension d (Table 1); matches the lower bound O(n*sqrt(alpha)) from Lai et al. 2016
Prior best (Sekhari et al. 2021, Newton step): capacity ~eOmega(n*min{alpha, sqrt(alpha)*epsilon^{1/4}/d^{1/4}}), i.e. decays as ~1/d^{1/4}, with time complexity O(n*d^2 + d^{2.38}) and space O(d^2)
DP-based 'lazy' unlearning (Huang & Canonne 2023): capacity eTheta(n*sqrt(alpha*epsilon/d)) (dimension-dependent), time Omega(n^2 d)

Algorithm 1 (GD) achieves training/unlearning time O(nd*log(...)) and space O(d); computational deletion capacity Omega(n * alpha*epsilon*exp(T/2nd) / (R*d*||theta0 - theta*_S||)) in the Lipschitz case

Proposition 2: for f=1 there exists a 1-strongly-convex, 1-smooth loss and an OOD forget sample making Algorithm 1's unlearning-time initialization error arbitrarily large (||theta*_{Sr U zf} - theta*_{Sr}||^2 = Delta for any Delta>0)

Out-of-distribution (Table 2): Algorithm 2 utility deletion capacity Omega(n); computational capacity Omega(n * alpha^2 epsilon^2 exp(T/nd) / (E(Sr) d^2 ||theta0 - theta*_{Sr}||^2)); Algorithm 1's non-Lipschitz OOD computational capacity is 0

Algorithm 2 unlearning time driven by retain-set interpolation error E(Sr) instead of the unbounded minimizer distance; empirically Algorithm 1 (non-robust) can be ~10x slower to unlearn than Algorithm 2 (Figure 1b)
Figure 1a: DP-SGD in-distribution error grows near-linearly with dimension d while Algorithm 1's error stays essentially flat (f=20 out of 10,000)
baselines_compared: Sekhari et al. (2021) -- Newton step + output perturbation (prior best utility deletion capacity)
Huang & Canonne (2023) / DP-SGD (Bassily et al. 2014) -- differential-privacy 'lazy' unlearning
Neel et al. (2021) descent-to-delete and Chourasia & Shah (2023) -- prior certified GD-based unlearning (shown by this paper to also achieve tight ID capacity)
Lower bounds from robust mean estimation (Lai et al. 2016; Diakonikolas et al. 2019) used to establish tightness
Marchant et al. (2022) -- slow-down / DoS poisoning attack that Algorithm 2 defends against
